content:
# Leviticus Chapter 10 — Disagreement Log

**Chapter:** Leviticus 10 (20 verses)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Agents:** EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic)

---

## Summary

Leviticus 10 generated a high density of disagreements — 54 distinct rendering differences across the 20 verses. The chapter's combination of dense narrative (vv. 1–7), direct divine speech (vv. 8–11), legal instruction (vv. 12–15), and a human dispute-and-resolution sequence (vv. 16–20) produced divergences at every level: technical cultic terms, Hebrew name forms (EMET consistently retaining Hebrew forms against series standard), word-order differences in legal prohibition clauses, the central rendering of Aaron's silence (v. 3), and the final closing idiom of v. 20. EMET caused the most divergences through bracket-heavy presentation and Hebrew name retention. PNEUMA caused divergences through dynamic compressions (especially vv. 20, 17, 19). LOGOS and EMET were in closest overall agreement on legal-technical terms. The most significant theological disagreements are at v. 3 (eqqadesh: "show myself holy" vs. "be sanctified"), v. 3 (qerovai: "draw near" vs. "near me"), v. 17 (nasa' avon: "bear the iniquity" vs. "remove the guilt"), and v. 20 (the final idiom).

---

## Verse-by-Verse Disagreement Table

### Verse 1

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Verse paragraph structure | Separate numbered paragraph; long bracketed sentence | Single paragraph with v. 2 | Single paragraph with v. 2 | **LOGOS/PNEUMA** — vv. 1–2 form a single rapid wayyiqtol narrative chain; paragraph break between them interrupts the verbal momentum |
| "fire-pan" vs. "censer" | "fire-pan" [machtah] | "censer" | "fire-pan" | **EMET/PNEUMA** — "fire-pan"; the machtah is a flat coal-carrying vessel, not a swinging censer; precision matters for understanding the nature of the priestly offense |
| "laid" vs. "placed" | "placed incense upon it" | "laid incense on it" | "laid incense on top" | **LOGOS/PNEUMA** — "laid incense on it"; natural English idiom for vayasimu in this context |
| "offered" vs. "brought near" | "brought near before the LORD esh zarah" | "offered unauthorized fire before the LORD" | "offered unauthorized fire before the LORD" | **LOGOS/PNEUMA** — "offered"; vayaqrivu (hiphil of qrv) in the cultic context is "offered"; "brought near" is overly literal |
| Em-dash construction | Long bracketed clause | Relative clause without dash | Dash before "fire he had not commanded them" | **PNEUMA** (adapted) — em-dash: "...offered unauthorized fire before the LORD — fire he had not commanded them" preserves the pause that marks the theological qualifier |
| Hebrew name forms | "Aharon," "Nadav," "Avihu" throughout | "Aaron," "Nadab," "Abihu" | "Aaron," "Nadab," "Abihu" | **LOGOS/PNEUMA** — English name forms are the series standard; EMET's Hebrew retention rejected throughout the chapter |

---

### Verse 2

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Opening waw-consecutive | "And fire came out from before the LORD" | "And fire came out from before the LORD" | "Fire came out from before the LORD" | **EMET/LOGOS** — "And fire came out"; the waw-consecutive (vattetse) must be retained to preserve the critical verbal parallel with Lev 9:24 |
| "consumed" vs. "consumed them" | "consumed them" [vattokal otam] | "consumed them" | "consumed them" | **All agree** — unanimous on "consumed them" |
| "they died before the LORD" | "and they died before the LORD" | "and they died before the LORD" | "and they died before the LORD" | **All agree** — unanimous |

---

### Verse 3

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Transition marker | "And Moshe said to Aharon" | "Then Moses said to Aaron" | "Moses said to Aaron" | **LOGOS** — "Then Moses said to Aaron"; "Then" marks the pivot from narrative to theological interpretation appropriately; EMET's "And" is flat; PNEUMA's bare opening loses the transition |
| Citation formula | "This is what the LORD spoke, saying" | "This is what the LORD has spoken" | "This is what the LORD meant when he said" | **EMET** (adapted) — "This is what the LORD spoke, saying"; hu' asher dibber YHWH lemor includes the quotative lemor which LOGOS omits; PNEUMA's "what the LORD meant when he said" is interpretively expansive and unjustified |
| eqqadesh (niphal of qadash) | "I will show myself holy" | "I will be sanctified" | "I will show myself holy" | **EMET/PNEUMA** — "show myself holy"; the niphal here is YHWH's self-manifestation in the act of judgment; "be sanctified" (LOGOS) implies something done to YHWH rather than YHWH's own self-disclosure |
| bi-qerovai (priestly near ones) | "Among those near me" | "Among those who are near me" | "Among those who come near me" | **PNEUMA** (adapted) — "Among those who draw near to me"; the qrv root is inherently dynamic (the priestly approach); PNEUMA's "come near" is closer than EMET's static "near me" or LOGOS's "who are near me"; "draw near" is adopted for the slightly more formal cultic register |
| ekkavedeh (I will be glorified) | "I will be glorified" | "I will be glorified" | "I will be glorified" | **All agree** — unanimous |
| "before all the people" | "before all the people" | "before all the people" | "before all the people" | **All agree** — unanimous |
| va-yiddom Aharon | "And Aharon was silent" | "And Aaron was silent" | "And Aaron was silent" | **LOGOS/PNEUMA** — "And Aaron was silent"; unanimous agreement on the substantive rendering; EMET's Hebrew name form rejected; LXX κατενύχθη ("was pierced with compunction") not adopted — MT restraint preserved |

---

### Verse 4

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "Come near" vs. "Draw near" vs. "Come forward" | "Draw near" | "Come near" | "Come forward" | **LOGOS** — "Come near"; natural spoken-command form of qirvu; "draw near" is overly formal; "come forward" changes directionality |
| "carry your brothers" vs. "carry your relatives" | "carry your brothers" | "carry your brothers" | "carry your relatives" | **EMET/LOGOS** — "carry your brothers"; acheykhem = "your brothers"; while Mishael and Elzaphan are cousins, the Hebrew term is 'ach and its literal rendering is preserved |
| "the front of the sanctuary" vs. "before the holy place" | "before the holy place" | "the front of the sanctuary" | "the front of the sanctuary" | **LOGOS/PNEUMA** — "the front of the sanctuary"; more natural English locative rendering of mi-lifney ha-qodesh |
| "Aaron's uncle" vs. "the uncle of Aaron" | "the uncle of Aharon" | "the uncle of Aaron" | "Aaron's uncle" | **PNEUMA** — "Aaron's uncle"; dod Aharon is rendered most naturally as a possessive in English |

---

### Verse 5

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "as Moses had said" vs. "as Moses had spoken" | "as Moshe had spoken" | "as Moses had said" | "as Moses had said" | **LOGOS/PNEUMA** — "as Moses had said"; ka-asher dibber Moshe; "said" is the natural English idiom; "spoken" (EMET) is accurate but slightly elevated for a narrative compliance formula |
| "outside the camp" placement | "to the outside of the camp" | "outside the camp" | "outside the camp" | **LOGOS/PNEUMA** — "outside the camp"; el-michutz la-machaneh is rendered naturally as "outside the camp" without the added "to the" |

---

### Verse 6

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Addressees syntax | "to Aharon and to Eleazar and to Itamar his sons" | "to Aaron and to Eleazar and Ithamar his sons" | "to Aaron and his sons Eleazar and Ithamar" | **LOGOS** — "to Aaron and to Eleazar and Ithamar his sons"; preserves the MT's syntactic individuation of Aaron as separate addressee while being less cumbersome than three "and-to" constructions (EMET); PNEUMA's restructuring loses the individuation |
| "let your hair hang loose" vs. "uncover your heads" | "Your heads you shall not uncover" | "Do not let your hair hang loose" | "Do not uncover your heads" | **LOGOS** — "Do not let your hair hang loose"; para' specifies the hair-dishevelment mourning gesture; EMET and PNEUMA follow the LXX's ἀποκαλύπτω (head-uncovering) rather than the MT's more specific para' |
| "lest" vs. "or" | "lest you die" | "lest you die" | "or you will die" | **EMET/LOGOS** — "lest you die"; pen is the warning-conditional; "or you will die" (PNEUMA) loses the conditional register |
| "wrath come upon all the congregation" | "upon all the congregation he be wrathful" | "wrath come upon all the congregation" | "anger will fall on the whole community" | **LOGOS** — "wrath come upon all the congregation"; yiqtsop is divine wrath breaking out; "wrath come upon" is the idiomatic English equivalent; EMET's "he be wrathful" is syntactically awkward; PNEUMA's "whole community" loses ha-edah (congregation) |
| "the whole house of Israel" | "all the house of Yisra'el" | "the whole house of Israel" | "the whole house of Israel" | **LOGOS/PNEUMA** — "the whole house of Israel"; kol beyt Yisra'el; "whole house" is the standard English rendering |
| "the burning that the LORD has kindled" | "the burning which the LORD has set ablaze" | "the burning that the LORD has kindled" | "the burning that the LORD has caused" | **LOGOS** — "the burning that the LORD has kindled"; ha-sreifah asher saraf YHWH; "kindled" preserves fire imagery while being idiomatic; "set ablaze" (EMET) is more vivid but slightly dramatic; "caused" (PNEUMA) loses the fire imagery of saraf entirely |

---

### Verse 7

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "from the entrance" vs. "from the entrance of" | "from the entrance of the tent of meeting" | "from the entrance of the tent of meeting" | "from the entrance of the tent of meeting" | **All agree** — unanimous |
| "according to the word of Moses" vs. "as Moses said" | "according to the word of Moses" | "according to the word of Moses" | "as Moses said" | **EMET/LOGOS** — "according to the word of Moses"; ki-devar Moshe is more precisely rendered with the full noun phrase; PNEUMA's compression is acceptable but loses the formal authority marker |

---

### Verse 8

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Verse paragraph treatment | Standalone verse paragraph | Merged into v. 9 speech | Merged into v. 9 speech | **EMET/LOGOS** (typographical) — v. 8 stands as an address-marker paragraph; the unique direct address to Aaron (not through Moses) is theologically significant and merits the visual separation |

---

### Verse 9

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Word order of prohibition | "Wine... and strong drink... you shall not drink — you and your sons with you — when you enter" | "Drink no wine or strong drink, you or your sons with you, when you go into" | "You and your sons are not to drink wine or strong drink when you enter" | **EMET** (adapted) — "Wine and strong drink you shall not drink"; MT topicalizes yayin ve-shekhar at the front of the clause for emphasis; "Wine and strong drink you shall not drink" preserves this fronting; LOGOS normalizes the English word order and loses the emphasis |
| "when you enter" vs. "when you go into" | "when you enter" | "when you go into" | "when you enter" | **EMET/PNEUMA** — "when you enter"; more natural English for entering a sacred enclosed space |
| "It is a statute forever" vs. "It shall be a statute forever" vs. "a permanent statute" | "it is a statute forever" [chuqqat olam] | "It shall be a statute forever" | "a permanent statute" | **EMET** — "It is a statute forever"; chuqqat olam hi' is a verbless declarative clause; "is" (present declarative) is the correct rendering; "shall be" (LOGOS) imports prospective nuance; "permanent statute" (PNEUMA) replaces olam ("forever") |

---

### Verse 10

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "to distinguish" vs. "you are to distinguish" | "to distinguish between the holy and the common" | "You are to distinguish between the holy and the common" | "You must distinguish between the holy and the common" | **LOGOS** — "You are to distinguish"; this verse continues as a purpose clause from v. 9; the governing "you are to" links it to the priestly duty; "you must" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but slightly imperatival without grammatical warrant |
| "the holy and the common" | "the holy and the common" | "the holy and the common" | "the holy and the common" | **All agree** — unanimous |
| "the unclean and the clean" | "the unclean and the clean" | "the unclean and the clean" | "the unclean and the clean" | **All agree** — unanimous |

---

### Verse 11

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| bney yisrael | "sons of Yisra'el" | "people of Israel" | "the Israelites" | **EMET** (adapted to English name) — "sons of Israel"; series standard; "people of Israel" (LOGOS) imprecise; "Israelites" (PNEUMA) loses the phrase structure |
| "through Moses" vs. "by Moses" | "through Moshe" | "by Moses" | "through Moses" | **EMET/PNEUMA** — "through Moses"; be-yad Moshe = "by the hand of Moses"; "through" captures the mediation sense; "by" (LOGOS) could imply direct Mosaic authorship |

---

### Verse 12

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "his remaining sons" | "the ones remaining" [ha-notarim] | "his remaining sons" | "his remaining sons Eleazar and Ithamar" | **LOGOS** (adapted) — "his remaining sons"; "the ones remaining" (EMET) is more literal but awkward as an English phrase; PNEUMA's explicit naming is acceptable but slightly redundant after v. 6's identification |
| "that remains" vs. "left over" | "that remains from the fire offerings" | "that remains from the LORD's fire offerings" | "left over from the LORD's fire offerings" | **LOGOS** — "that remains from the LORD's fire offerings"; relative-clause rendering is slightly more precise than appositive "left over" |
| "beside the altar" vs. "next to the altar" | "beside the altar" | "beside the altar" | "beside the altar" | **All agree** — unanimous |

---

### Verse 13

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "for it is your due and your sons' due" | "for it is your due and the due of your sons" | "because it is your due and your sons' due" | "because it is your portion and your sons' portion" | **EMET** (adapted) — "for it is your due and your sons' due"; ki is causal "for"; "due" renders choq (decreed portion) over "portion" (PNEUMA) which is acceptable but less specific |
| "for so I have been commanded" | "for thus I have been commanded" | "for so I am commanded" | "that is what I have been commanded" | **EMET** (adapted) — "for so I have been commanded"; tsuvveyti is passive perfect; "have been commanded" (EMET) is more accurate than present "am commanded" (LOGOS); PNEUMA's "that is what" is idiomatic but slightly wordy |

---

### Verse 14

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "shall eat" vs. "may eat" | "you shall eat... in a clean place" | "you shall eat in a clean place" | "you may eat in any clean place" | **EMET/LOGOS** — "you shall eat in a clean place"; the lesser-holy portions may be eaten in any clean place, but the obligation to eat them is still mandatory; "may eat" (PNEUMA) makes it permissive when it is required; "any clean place" (PNEUMA) is interpretively accurate but adds "any" not in the MT |
| bney yisrael | "the sons of Yisra'el" | "the people of Israel" | "the Israelites" | **EMET** (adapted) — "the sons of Israel"; series standard |

---

### Verse 15

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "as a due forever" vs. "as your and your children's permanent portion" | "as a due forever" | "as a due forever" | "as your and your children's permanent portion" | **EMET/LOGOS** — "as a due forever"; le-choq olam; "forever" renders olam; PNEUMA substitutes both key terms |

---

### Verse 16

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "diligently sought" vs. "diligently inquired about" vs. "searched carefully for" | "diligently sought" [darosh darash] | "diligently inquired about" | "searched carefully for" | **EMET** — "diligently sought"; darosh darash is the doubled intensive; "sought" is direct and preserves both the urgency and the investigative-legal nuance of darash; "inquired about" (LOGOS) imports a connotation of asking others; "searched carefully for" (PNEUMA) loses the legal-inquiry nuance |
| "and behold" | "and behold" | "and behold" | PNEUMA omits "behold" | **EMET/LOGOS** — "and behold"; hinneh marks Moses' discovery as a sudden realization and should be retained |
| "burned up" vs. "burned" | "it had been burned" | "it had been burned up" | "it had already been burned up" | **LOGOS** — "it had been burned up"; saraf as a completed fact; "already been burned up" (PNEUMA) adds "already" without MT support |
| "remaining sons" vs. "surviving sons" | "the remaining ones" [ha-notarim] | "the surviving sons of Aaron" | "Aaron's surviving sons" | **EMET** (adapted) — "the remaining sons of Aaron"; consistent with v. 12 ruling; "surviving" is not wrong but "remaining" (yatar) is more faithful to the Hebrew and avoids the implication of escape from danger |

---

### Verse 17

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "in the holy place" vs. "in the place of the sanctuary" | "in the holy place" [ba-maqom ha-qodesh] | "in the place of the sanctuary" | "in the holy place" | **EMET/PNEUMA** — "in the holy place"; ba-maqom ha-qodesh is the standard term; "the place of the sanctuary" (LOGOS) is more expansive but paraphrase-like |
| "since it is" vs. "for it is" | "For it is most holy" | "since it is a thing most holy" | "It is most holy" | **EMET** — "For it is most holy"; ki is causal "for"; LOGOS's "since" is acceptable but slightly more explanatory; PNEUMA's rendering as a bare assertion without a connector loses the causal relationship to the "why" of Moses' question |
| "bear the iniquity" vs. "remove the guilt" | "to bear the iniquity of the congregation" | "that you may bear the iniquity of the congregation" | "to remove the guilt of the community" | **EMET/LOGOS** — "to bear the iniquity of the congregation"; la-set = nasa' (to bear/carry); "remove" (PNEUMA) follows LXX ἀφαιρέω and shifts from the action to its result; "iniquity" ('avon) retained over "guilt"; "congregation" (ha-edah) retained over "community" |

---

### Verse 18

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "Behold" | "Behold" [hen] | "Behold" | omitted | **EMET/LOGOS** — "Behold"; hen is a deictic particle that introduces the evidence for Moses' argument and should be retained |
| "into the inner sanctuary" vs. "into the inner part of the sanctuary" vs. "inside the sanctuary" | "into the inner sanctuary" | "into the inner part of the sanctuary" | "inside the sanctuary" | **EMET** — "into the inner sanctuary"; el-ha-qodesh (with panimah "within" implied by context); "the inner sanctuary" captures the technical distinction between outer court and inner tent without being wordy; "inside the sanctuary" (PNEUMA) is too vague for this legally precise context |
| "You certainly should have eaten it in the holy place" | "you certainly should have eaten it" [with "in the holy place"] | "You certainly ought to have eaten it in the sanctuary" | "you absolutely should have eaten it in the sanctuary" | **EMET** (adapted) — "You certainly should have eaten it in the holy place"; akol tokhal otah is the infinitive absolute construction for emphatic certainty; "certainly should have" renders the emphasis; "ought to have" (LOGOS) is slightly weaker; "absolutely should have" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but slightly colloquial; ba-qodesh = "in the holy place" (series standard) |

---

### Verse 19

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "And Aaron spoke" vs. "And Aaron said" | "And Aharon spoke to Moshe" | "And Aaron said to Moses" | "Aaron spoke to Moses" | **EMET** (adapted to English names) — "And Aaron spoke to Moses"; vayedabber is the slightly more formal speech verb; "spoke" is the appropriate rendering; "And" preserves the waw-consecutive |
| "Behold — today" | "Behold — today" | "Behold, today" | "Look — today" | **EMET** (adapted) — "Behold — today"; hen with the em-dash creates the pause that matches Aaron's careful, controlled speech; "Look" (PNEUMA) is too colloquial for this context |
| "such things as these have happened to me" | "things like these have happened to me" [ka-eleh] | "such things as these have happened to me" | "these things have happened to me" | **LOGOS** — "such things as these have happened to me"; ka-eleh = "things like these / such things as these"; LOGOS preserves both the indefinite reference and the restraint of Aaron's understatement; PNEUMA's "these things" is too specific and loses the deliberate vagueness of ka-eleh |
| "would it have been pleasing" vs. "would it have been acceptable" | "would it have been acceptable in the eyes of the LORD?" | "would it have been pleasing in the sight of the LORD?" | "would the LORD have approved?" | **LOGOS** — "would it have been pleasing in the sight of the LORD?"; ha-yitav be-eyney YHWH; "pleasing" renders yatav well; eye-idiom "in the sight of" is retained and will be echoed in v. 20; "acceptable" (EMET) is slightly legalistic; "would the LORD have approved?" (PNEUMA) is idiomatic but discards both the idiom and the verbal root shared with v. 20 |

---

### Verse 20

| Feature | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Full idiom vs. compressed rendering | "And Moshe heard and it was acceptable in his eyes" | "And when Moses heard that, he approved" | "Moses heard this and approved" | **EMET** (adapted to series standards) — "And Moses heard, and it was pleasing in his sight"; vayyishma' Moshe vayyitav be-eynav; the two-clause waw-consecutive structure is preserved; "pleasing in his sight" echoes Aaron's question in v. 19 (ha-yitav be-eyney YHWH); this echo is the theological point of the closing — Aaron asked if it would be "pleasing in the sight of the LORD," and Moses finds it "pleasing in his sight" (Moses as the LORD's authorized spokesman accepting the defense); both LOGOS and PNEUMA's "he approved" or "approved" are too compressed and destroy the verbal echo |
| "And Moses heard" vs. "when Moses heard that" | "And Moshe heard" | "And when Moses heard that" | "Moses heard this" | **EMET** (adapted) — "And Moses heard"; the waw-consecutive is a simple sequence marker; "when Moses heard that" (LOGOS) imports a temporal subordination not in the MT's simple wayyishma' |

---

## Aggregate Disagreement Statistics

| Category | Count |
|---|---|
| Hebrew name forms (EMET vs. series standard) | 8 |
| Word-order / topicalization differences | 4 |
| Technical cultic term rendering | 7 |
| Compression vs. full rendering (PNEUMA) | 6 |
| Connector / transition words (And/Then/Now/bare) | 5 |
| Idiom preservation vs. modernization | 6 |
| Theological rendering differences (eqqadesh, nasa' avon, va-yiddom) | 3 |
| LXX-influenced renderings rejected | 3 |
| Paragraph / section structure | 4 |
| Pronoun and minor word variation | 8 |
| **Total distinct disagreements** | **54** |

---

## Most Significant Theological Disagreements

### 1. Verse 3 — eqqadesh: "I will show myself holy" vs. "I will be sanctified"

LOGOS renders the niphal of qadash as "I will be sanctified," implying something done to YHWH. EMET and PNEUMA render it as "I will show myself holy," preserving the reflexive-resultative sense of the niphal — YHWH's holiness is manifested through judgment. SOPHIA rules for "show myself holy" (EMET/PNEUMA): in this context, the niphal eqqadesh denotes YHWH's active self-disclosure through the consuming fire, not a passive reception of sanctification from others. The LXX's ἁγιασθήσομαι (future passive "I will be sanctified") supports LOGOS's reading, but the contextual force of the verse — divine fire as self-revelation — favors the active-reflexive rendering.

### 2. Verse 17 — la-set et-avon ha-edah: "bear the iniquity" vs. "remove the guilt"

PNEUMA renders la-set (nasa') as "remove the guilt," following the LXX's ἀφαιρέω ("take away"). EMET and LOGOS retain "bear the iniquity." The distinction is theologically significant: "bear" preserves the sacrificial-priestly mechanism by which the priest takes the congregation's sin onto himself (as a personal burden) through the act of eating; "remove" interprets the result rather than the action. The eating-as-bearing theology is central to understanding Moses' anger in vv. 16–18. SOPHIA rules for "bear the iniquity" (EMET/LOGOS).

### 3. Verse 20 — vayyitav be-eynav: Full idiom vs. "he approved"

All three agents render v. 20's closing differently. The full Hebrew idiom vayyitav be-eynav ("and it was good/pleasing in his eyes") creates a deliberate verbal echo with Aaron's question in v. 19 (ha-yitav be-eyney YHWH — "would it have been pleasing in the eyes/sight of the LORD?"). Aaron asks whether eating would have been "pleasing in the sight of the LORD"; Moses finds the defense "pleasing in his sight." The chapter's resolution turns precisely on this echo. Compressing v. 20 to "he approved" (LOGOS) or "approved" (PNEUMA) destroys the verbal parallel and thereby flattens the theological point. SOPHIA rules for the full idiom rendering.

### 4. Verse 3 — va-yiddom Aharon: MT silence vs. LXX emotional interpretation

EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA all agree on "Aaron was silent" for va-yiddom Aharon — all three reject the LXX's κατενύχθη ("was pierced with compunction") in the translated text itself. This is a rare unanimous agreement that goes against the LXX. The MT's restraint is the stronger literary and theological choice: the raw, unadorned silence carries more weight than any emotional interpretation. SOPHIA affirms this unanimous decision.

— SOPHIA
